description:
You are a script-developer who keeps all source scripts under “/home/user/dev/bin”.  
For daily convenience you want symbolic links to those scripts inside “/home/user/bin”.

Current situation (already present when the session starts):

• The directory “/home/user/dev/bin” exists and contains two executable files:  
  – /home/user/dev/bin/run_backup.sh  
  – /home/user/dev/bin/check_status.sh  

• There is an **out-of-date** symbolic link called “run_backup” inside “/home/user/bin” that points to “/usr/local/bin/run_backup_old”.

Goal – bring your personal bin directory up-to-date:

1. Make sure the directory “/home/user/bin” exists (create it if it does not).  
2. Delete the obsolete symlink “/home/user/bin/run_backup” (only if it exists).  
3. Create two new symbolic links:  
   • /home/user/bin/run_backup   → /home/user/dev/bin/run_backup.sh  
   • /home/user/bin/check_status → /home/user/dev/bin/check_status.sh  
   (Links must be absolute, exactly as written above.)

4. Produce a plain-text log file “/home/user/symlink_creation.log”.  
   • The file must contain **exactly two lines**, one per link.  
   • Each line must have this exact format:  
     <absolute-path-to-symlink><single space>-><single space><absolute-path-to-target>  
   • The order of the lines must be: first the run_backup line, then the check_status line.  
   • Example of the required final content (do NOT add extra spaces or trailing newline after the second line):

     /home/user/bin/run_backup -> /home/user/dev/bin/run_backup.sh  
     /home/user/bin/check_status -> /home/user/dev/bin/check_status.sh

The automated evaluation script will:

• Verify that “/home/user/bin/run_backup” and “/home/user/bin/check_status” exist and are valid symbolic links pointing to the correct targets.  
• Confirm that “/home/user/bin/run_backup” no longer points to “/usr/local/bin/run_backup_old”.  
• Check that the log file “/home/user/symlink_creation.log” exists and its content matches the two-line format shown above exactly (no extra blank lines, no additional text).

You can use any Linux command-line utilities to accomplish the task, but it should require no more than a small handful of commands (typically 2–3).
truth:
Initial filesystem state before the agent starts:

Directories:
  /home/user/dev
  /home/user/dev/bin
  /home/user/bin        (may or may not already exist, but if it exists it already holds an incorrect symlink)
Files already present:
  /home/user/dev/bin/run_backup.sh        (executable; contents irrelevant for testing)
  /home/user/dev/bin/check_status.sh      (executable; contents irrelevant for testing)

Incorrect symlink that exists at start:
  /home/user/bin/run_backup  →  /usr/local/bin/run_backup_old

No other files inside /home/user/bin.

Expected final state after the agent finishes:

1. Directory /home/user/bin exists.
2. Symbolic links:
   • /home/user/bin/run_backup   → /home/user/dev/bin/run_backup.sh
   • /home/user/bin/check_status → /home/user/dev/bin/check_status.sh
   Both must resolve correctly; /home/user/bin/run_backup must *not* point anywhere else.
3. Log file /home/user/symlink_creation.log exists with exactly the following bytes
   (two lines, newline after the first line only):

/home/user/bin/run_backup -> /home/user/dev/bin/run_backup.sh
/home/user/bin/check_status -> /home/user/dev/bin/check_status.sh

4. The old symlink /home/user/bin/run_backup that pointed to /usr/local/bin/run_backup_old has been replaced (link target updated).
